enrichment.short_description: An open-source PHP framework for building custom social networking and community platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Elgg is an open-source social networking engine, originally created in 2004 by Ben Werdmuller and Dave Tosh, that developers use as a framework or toolkit to build custom community platforms rather than a turnkey social network. Governance moved to the nonprofit Elgg Foundation in 2010, and the project has continued through major PHP-based version releases since, with version 7 as its current line.
Rather than shipping a fixed set of social features, Elgg provides a plugin, entity, and event-hook architecture that developers extend to fit a specific community's needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Elgg is aimed at developers and organizations — universities, NGOs, government agencies, and enterprises — that need to build a bespoke social or community platform, such as intranets, communities of practice, or learning networks, and want a modular, extensible PHP foundation rather than starting from scratch.

features.title: Plugin/extension architecture
features.description: A modular plugin system with a large library of community-built plugins.

features.title: Composer-based packaging
features.description: Dependencies and plugins are managed through Composer.

features.title: Entity system
features.description: A flexible data model for defining custom content types.

features.title: Access control & groups
features.description: Fine-grained, per-item access permissions and built-in user groups.

features.title: Event & hook system
features.description: Developers can tap into Elgg's event and view hooks to customize behavior without modifying core.

features.title: File storage & notifications
features.description: Built-in file handling and a notification system.

features.title: Internationalization
features.description: Core support for translating the platform into multiple languages.

features.title: Security protections
features.description: Built-in CSRF and XSS protections.

pros: Free and open source, dual-licensed under GPLv2 and MIT
Highly extensible plugin and event architecture suited to building bespoke community platforms
Long track record since 2004 with a stable nonprofit steward, the Elgg Foundation
Strong access-control model for building platforms with complex privacy requirements
cons: It's a framework, not an out-of-the-box social network — real use requires development effort to configure plugins and design an experience
PHP/MySQL stack and smaller community than newer JavaScript-based community platforms
Has a learning curve around its plugin, event, and hook system for new developers

faqs.question: Is Elgg free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Elgg is free and open source, dual-licensed under GPLv2 and MIT.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use Elgg?
faqs.answer: Elgg is a development framework — building a full community site typically requires PHP development or configuring existing community plugins.

faqs.question: What does Elgg run on?
faqs.answer: Elgg is written in PHP and uses a MySQL-compatible database, running on a standard Apache or Nginx web server.

faqs.question: Who maintains Elgg?
faqs.answer: The nonprofit Elgg Foundation, which took over stewardship in 2010.

use_cases.title: Organizational intranets
use_cases.description: Companies and NGOs build internal community and knowledge-sharing platforms on top of Elgg's plugin system.

use_cases.title: Educational communities of practice
use_cases.description: Universities and training organizations use Elgg to build learning-focused social platforms.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Elgg is a free, open-source PHP framework for building custom social networking and community platforms, maintained by the Elgg Foundation.

enrichment.short_description: A modern, cross-platform bulletin board system server that recreates the classic dial-up terminal community experience over Telnet and SSH.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ENiGMA½ (ENiGMA 1/2) is an open-source BBS server, first published on GitHub in 2015 by developer NuSkooler, that lets operators run a modern, multi-node terminal community reachable over Telnet, SSH, and WebSockets. It brings the retro dial-up bulletin board format — message boards, file areas, door games — to contemporary server infrastructure built on Node.js.
The project remains actively maintained, with a large commit history and ongoing releases, and is highly configurable through JavaScript-based menus, themes, and "mods."

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ENiGMA½ is built for BBS hobbyists, sysops, and retrocomputing/ANSI-art communities who want to run a multi-node terminal bulletin board rather than a web-based forum.

features.title: Multi-protocol access
features.description: Supports Telnet, SSH, and WebSocket connections for terminal clients.

features.title: ANSI/ASCII art support
features.description: Renders ANSI, CP437, and UTF-8 art, including SAUCE metadata.

features.title: FidoNet/BinkP networking
features.description: Can participate in FidoNet-style inter-BBS message networks over BinkP.

features.title: Door game support
features.description: Runs classic DOS door games via built-in x86 emulation, plus Z-Machine interactive fiction.

features.title: File areas
features.description: A searchable file base with HTTP download links for uploaded files.

features.title: Customizable menus & mods
features.description: JavaScript-based menu system and "mods" for building custom BBS experiences and themes.

features.title: Security
features.description: PBKDF2 password hashing with two-factor authentication support.

features.title: Built-in extra servers
features.description: Includes its own HTTPS web server plus Gopher and NNTP servers.

pros: Free and open source under the BSD-2-Clause license
Actively maintained, with a large commit history and recent releases
Rich protocol support spanning Telnet, SSH, WebSocket, FidoNet, Gopher, and NNTP, beyond a typical single-purpose BBS
Highly customizable via a JavaScript-based menu and mod system
cons: Aimed at a niche, retro-computing audience rather than mainstream community-building use cases
Requires comfort running and administering a Node.js server
No polished graphical installer — setup is config-file driven (HJSON)

faqs.question: Is ENiGMA½ free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the BSD-2-Clause license.

faqs.question: What do I need to run it?
faqs.answer: A server running Node.js; data is stored in SQLite, and configuration is done through HJSON files.

faqs.question: Can users connect the same way they did to classic BBSes?
faqs.answer: Yes — ENiGMA½ supports Telnet and SSH connections in addition to modern WebSocket access.

faqs.question: Does it support door games?
faqs.answer: Yes, including native DOS door game emulation and Z-Machine interactive fiction support.

use_cases.title: Retro BBS communities
use_cases.description: Hobbyists stand up a classic-style multi-node bulletin board reachable over Telnet/SSH for message boards and file sharing.

use_cases.title: FidoNet-connected boards
use_cases.description: Operators link their board into the wider FidoNet message network using built-in BinkP support.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ENiGMA half BBS is a free, open-source BBS server supporting Telnet, SSH, and WebSocket access, ANSI art, door games, and FidoNet networking.

enrichment.short_description: A lightweight, self-hosted ActivityPub server that provides Mastodon-API-compatible federated social networking with a small resource footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GoToSocial is an open-source ActivityPub server, started as a solo project around 2021 by a small team known as Super Serious Business, designed to be a lightweight alternative to Mastodon for running a single-user or small-group fediverse server. It's written in Go and is explicitly built around the idea of running "many small, weird, specialist servers" rather than large centralized instances.
The project has been in beta since late 2024 and continues active development toward a stable release, with its canonical codebase hosted on Codeberg.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: GoToSocial suits individuals and small communities who want to self-host a fediverse presence with minimal server resources, without the algorithmic-feed and large-instance dynamics of bigger platforms.

features.title: Chronological timelines
features.description: No algorithmic ranking; posts appear in time order.

features.title: Granular visibility controls
features.description: Public, unlisted, followers-only, and direct-message-style post visibility.

features.title: Reply-interaction policies
features.description: Fine-grained control over who can reply to a given post.

features.title: Mastodon-API compatibility
features.description: Existing Mastodon-compatible mobile and desktop apps can connect to a GoToSocial account.

features.title: Markdown post formatting
features.description: Posts can be composed using Markdown.

features.title: Theming
features.description: Custom CSS and profile theming options.

features.title: Built-in HTTPS
features.description: Automatic HTTPS/Let's Encrypt certificate handling.

features.title: OIDC integration
features.description: Supports OpenID Connect for external authentication.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPLv3 license
Very low resource requirements, roughly 250-350MB of RAM, making it cheap to self-host on small VPS instances
Compatible with existing Mastodon apps via its Mastodon-API-compatible REST API
Actively developed with frequent releases and a small, focused core team
cons: Still in beta as of its current release cycle, not yet a "1.0" stable release
Smaller feature set than Mastodon by design, such as no nested boosting and limited discovery tools
No built-in algorithmic discovery, which reflects the project's philosophy but may not suit everyone

faqs.question: Is GoToSocial free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Can I use Mastodon apps with GoToSocial?
faqs.answer: Yes — GoToSocial implements a Mastodon-API-compatible REST API, so most existing Mastodon apps work with it.

faqs.question: Is GoToSocial stable and production-ready?
faqs.answer: It's in active beta; the project has not yet declared a 1.0 stable release.

faqs.question: What database does GoToSocial use?
faqs.answer: SQLite by default, with optional PostgreSQL support.

use_cases.title: Personal fediverse server
use_cases.description: An individual runs their own single-user ActivityPub server with a Mastodon-compatible app instead of joining a large instance.
use_cases.audience: Individuals wanting an independent fediverse presence

use_cases.title: Small-group or community instances
use_cases.description: Small communities run a low-resource federated server for their members without operating a full Mastodon deployment.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: GoToSocial is a free, open-source, lightweight ActivityPub server with a Mastodon-compatible API, built for small fediverse instances.

enrichment.short_description: A self-hosted bridge that adds automated ActivityPub/Fediverse federation to any static blog or website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hatsu is an open-source tool, with its GitHub repository created in mid-2023, that lets owners of static sites and blogs gain Fediverse followers and replies without building ActivityPub federation into their site themselves. It reads a site's existing RSS, Atom, or JSON feed as its data source and handles the federation protocol work — accepting follows, pushing new posts to followers, and feeding Fediverse replies back to the site — as a self-hosted, Docker-deployable service.
The project's own site describes Hatsu as still a work in progress, and it is explicitly scoped to ActivityPub rather than other decentralized-social protocols like Nostr or Bluesky's AT Protocol.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Hatsu is for static-site and blog owners on any site generator or static host who want their content to reach Mastodon and other ActivityPub-based Fediverse users without adopting a full dynamic CMS or federation-capable platform.

features.title: Feed-based federation
features.description: Consumes a site's RSS, Atom, or JSON feed to detect new posts and federate them automatically.

features.title: Follow handling
features.description: Accepts and manages Fediverse follow requests on behalf of the static site.

features.title: Reply backfeed
features.description: Captures replies made from the Fediverse and surfaces them back to the site.

features.title: Fediverse discovery redirects
features.description: Resolves Fediverse handle and URL lookups to the correct Hatsu-hosted resource.

features.title: Static-site agnostic
features.description: Works with any static site generator or static hosting provider, since it only needs a feed URL.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Ships as a self-hosted, Docker-deployable service.

features.title: ActivityPub-focused
features.description: Implements ActivityPub federation specifically; it does not bridge to Nostr or AT Protocol/Bluesky.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license
Requires no changes to an existing static site beyond having a syndication feed
Written in Rust and designed to be lightweight to self-host
Actively maintained with recent commits
cons: Explicitly labeled a work in progress by its own maintainers, so expect rough edges
Supports ActivityPub only, not other decentralized-social protocols
Smaller community and ecosystem than more established fediverse tools like GoToSocial or Mastodon
Requires self-hosting know-how, including Docker and DNS/HTTPS setup, to federate correctly

faqs.question: Is Hatsu free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Do I need to change my static site to use Hatsu?
faqs.answer: No — Hatsu only needs your site's existing RSS, Atom, or JSON feed to detect and federate new posts.

faqs.question: What protocol does Hatsu support?
faqs.answer: ActivityPub, the protocol used by Mastodon and most of the Fediverse; it does not support Nostr or Bluesky's AT Protocol.

faqs.question: Is Hatsu production-ready?
faqs.answer: The project's own documentation describes it as still a work in progress, so expect ongoing changes.

use_cases.title: Static blog federation
use_cases.description: Bloggers on Hugo, Jekyll, Eleventy, or similar static generators can gain Fediverse followers and replies by pointing Hatsu at their feed.

use_cases.title: Fediverse reach without a CMS switch
use_cases.description: Sites that don't want to move to a dynamic, federation-native CMS can still reach Mastodon and ActivityPub users.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hatsu is a free, open-source, self-hosted bridge that adds ActivityPub/Fediverse federation to any static blog using its existing feed.

enrichment.short_description: A decentralized social networking and publishing platform with nomadic identity that lets accounts move or clone across independently run servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hubzilla traces its lineage to Mike Macgirvin's 2010 "Mistpark"/Friendika work, which was forked into "Red"/"RedMatrix" in 2012 and renamed Hubzilla in 2015. It combines social networking, blogging, wikis, and cloud file storage into one federated platform, built around a distinctive "nomadic identity" system that lets a user's account be cloned or moved between independently run Hubzilla servers, called hubs, without losing their connections.
Development has been community-led since 2015, with the codebase hosted on Framagit and ongoing releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Hubzilla suits communities and organizations that want an all-in-one federated platform combining a social feed, file storage, wikis, and publishing, with strong, granular privacy controls, rather than a single-purpose microblogging server.

features.title: Nomadic identity
features.description: User identities can be cloned or relocated across different Hubzilla servers while retaining connections and content.

features.title: Fine-grained permissions
features.description: Per-post, per-file, or per-group access control rather than simple public/private toggles.

features.title: Federated single sign-on
features.description: Authentication that carries across federated hubs.

features.title: Built-in blogging & wikis
features.description: Native long-form publishing and collaborative wiki pages alongside social posts.

features.title: Cloud file storage
features.description: Permission-based file storage and sharing built into the platform.

features.title: Discussion groups
features.description: Built-in group discussion spaces.

features.title: Multi-protocol federation
features.description: Interoperates with ActivityPub, Diaspora, and its own Zot protocol, reaching users on Mastodon, Misskey, PeerTube, and other federated platforms.

features.title: Content filtering
features.description: Tools for filtering and blocking unwanted content or contacts.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Broader feature set than single-purpose fediverse tools, combining social, storage, wikis, and blogging in one platform
Nomadic identity is a distinctive feature that avoids full lock-in to any one server operator
Interoperates with multiple federation protocols, Zot, ActivityPub, and Diaspora, reaching a wide range of fediverse platforms
cons: Requires PHP/MySQL-compatible hosting, a steeper setup than some newer Go-based fediverse tools
Smaller mindshare and community than Mastodon or other more mainstream fediverse platforms
The breadth of features spanning social, storage, wikis, and blogging can mean a steeper learning curve for new users and admins
Community-led development pace can be slower than commercially backed alternatives

faqs.question: Is Hubzilla free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What is nomadic identity?
faqs.answer: It's Hubzilla's system for letting a user's account, connections, and content be cloned or moved to a different Hubzilla server without starting over.

faqs.question: What do I need to self-host Hubzilla?
faqs.answer: A PHP environment with a MySQL-compatible database, such as MariaDB, and a standard web server like Apache.

faqs.question: Does Hubzilla work with Mastodon?
faqs.answer: Yes — Hubzilla supports ActivityPub federation alongside its own Zot protocol, so it can interact with Mastodon, Misskey, PeerTube, and other ActivityPub-based platforms.

use_cases.title: All-in-one community hub
use_cases.description: Organizations run a single federated platform that combines social feed, file storage, wikis, and blogging for their members.

use_cases.title: Portable social identity
use_cases.description: Users who want to avoid being locked into one server operator can use nomadic identity to move their account between hubs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hubzilla is a free, open-source federated platform combining social networking, blogging, wikis, and cloud storage with nomadic identity.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted ActivityPub microblogging server built from scratch in .NET for performance and maintainability.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Iceshrimp.NET is a complete rewrite of the earlier Iceshrimp-JS project, built on a new .NET backend with a Blazor WASM frontend. It is a decentralized, self-hosted microblogging server that speaks ActivityPub, letting an instance federate with Mastodon, Misskey, Akkoma and the wider fediverse.

The project positions itself as a community-centric alternative to the larger ActivityPub server projects, with a stated focus on performance, resource efficiency and long-term maintainability rather than rapid feature sprawl. It ships a Mastodon-compatible API so existing third-party apps such as Elk, Tusky and Ice Cubes can connect to an instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Server administrators who already run or want to run their own fediverse instance and prefer a lighter, more efficient backend than existing ActivityPub servers, plus users who want to migrate an existing Iceshrimp-JS community onto a more maintainable codebase.
The project explicitly does not run a flagship public instance, so it's aimed at operators willing to self-host rather than end users looking for a ready-made account to sign up to.

features.title: ActivityPub federation
features.description: Interoperates with Mastodon, Misskey, Akkoma and other fediverse servers over the open ActivityPub standard.

features.title: Mastodon-compatible API
features.description: Supports many existing Mastodon-client apps (Elk, Tusky, Ice Cubes and others) without requiring a dedicated client.

features.title: .NET backend
features.description: Rewritten from the ground up in C# for improved performance and resource efficiency compared to the original Iceshrimp-JS.

features.title: Blazor WASM frontend
features.description: Web client built with Blazor WebAssembly, served directly from the instance.

features.title: Configurable public preview
features.description: Instance operators can configure what an anonymous HTML preview of posts looks like to non-federated visitors and crawlers.

features.title: Migration tooling
features.description: Provides a path for existing Iceshrimp-JS instances to migrate their data to Iceshrimp.NET.

pros: Free, open-source and self-hosted with no vendor lock-in
Performance-focused rewrite aimed at lower resource usage
Federates with the broader ActivityPub fediverse
Compatible with popular existing Mastodon client apps
Documented migration path from Iceshrimp-JS
cons: Still beta software with no official flagship instance to try first
Requires self-hosting and server administration skills
Smaller ecosystem and community than Mastodon or Misskey

faqs.question: Is Iceshrimp.NET free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is fully open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Can I use my existing Mastodon app with Iceshrimp.NET?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes a Mastodon-compatible API, so apps like Elk, Tusky and Ice Cubes can connect to an instance.

faqs.question: Is there an official public instance to join?
faqs.answer: No, the project does not operate a flagship instance; you need to self-host or find a community-run instance.

faqs.question: What is Iceshrimp.NET a rewrite of?
faqs.answer: It is a from-scratch rewrite of the earlier Iceshrimp-JS project, moving the backend to .NET.

use_cases.description: Move an existing Iceshrimp-JS community onto a more performant, maintainable backend.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, self-hosted comment server written in Python, built as a privacy-friendly alternative to Disqus.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Isso is a small, self-hosted commenting server that site owners embed into blogs and articles instead of using a third-party service like Disqus. The server is written in Python and stores comments in a SQLite database, keeping the deployment footprint small.
The embeddable client is a single JavaScript file (around 65KB, 20KB gzipped), which keeps page-weight impact low compared to many commercial commenting widgets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Bloggers and site operators who want comments on their static or dynamic sites without sending visitor data to a third-party ad-supported platform, and who are comfortable self-hosting a small Python service.

features.title: Markdown comments
features.description: Visitors can format comments using Markdown.

features.title: Self-edit and delete window
features.description: Commenters can edit or delete their own comment within a configurable time window (15 minutes by default).

features.title: Moderation queue
features.description: New comments can be held for moderator approval before appearing publicly.

features.title: Disqus and WordPress import
features.description: Existing comment threads can be migrated in from Disqus or WordPress.

features.title: Minimal JS client
features.description: A single small JavaScript file embeds the comment widget on any page.

features.title: SQLite storage
features.description: No separate database server is required; comments are stored in a SQLite file.

pros: Free and fully open source
Very small JavaScript footprint on embedding pages
No external database dependency to manage
Straightforward migration from Disqus or WordPress comments
Keeps visitor comment data off third-party platforms
cons: Requires self-hosting a Python service, not a plug-and-play SaaS
Fewer built-in integrations than commercial commenting platforms
No official managed hosting option

faqs.question: Is Isso free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Isso is open-source software and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What database does Isso use?
faqs.answer: Isso stores comments in a local SQLite database, so no separate database server is needed.

faqs.question: Can I migrate comments from Disqus?
faqs.answer: Yes, Isso provides import support for existing Disqus and WordPress comment data.

faqs.question: What browsers does the comment widget support?
faqs.answer: The embeddable JavaScript client supports Firefox, Safari, Chrome and Edge.

use_cases.title: Blog comments without third-party tracking

use_cases.title: Replacing Disqus

use_cases.audience: Site owners moving away from ad-supported comment platforms

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Isso is a free, open-source, self-hosted commenting server for blogs, offering Markdown comments and a lightweight JavaScript embed.

enrichment.short_description: Modern, self-hosted forum platform built with Django and React, designed to be fast, scalable and customizable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Misago is an open-source forum application built on Django with a React frontend, started by developer Rafał Pitoń in 2012. It aims to be a modern alternative to older PHP forum software, with Docker-based deployment, a plugin-friendly admin control panel, and a responsive interface.
The project is actively maintained, with ongoing releases and a community forum where translation, styling and plugin discussions happen alongside support threads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Community managers and developers who want a self-hosted discussion forum with a modern JavaScript frontend and are comfortable deploying and customizing a Django application, typically via Docker.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Ships with Docker support for streamlined installation and updates.

features.title: Admin control panel
features.description: Web-based ACP for managing users, permissions and forum settings.

features.title: Theming and branding
features.description: Custom styling and page header/branding options.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Extensible via plugins for adding functionality beyond the core.

features.title: User permission system
features.description: Granular permission groups control what members can see and do.

features.title: Internationalization
features.description: Interface has been translated into languages beyond English, including German.

pros: Free and open source with an active GitHub project
Modern React-based frontend rather than legacy PHP templating
Docker support simplifies self-hosted deployment
Plugin system for extending functionality
Active community forum for support and translations
cons: Requires comfort running a Django/Python stack to self-host
Smaller plugin ecosystem than long-established forum software like phpBB or SMF
No official managed/hosted version

faqs.question: Is Misago free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Misago is open-source software, free to download and self-host.

faqs.question: What tech stack does Misago use?
faqs.answer: A Django (Python) backend with a React frontend.

faqs.question: Can I deploy Misago with Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project supports Docker-based deployment.

faqs.question: Does Misago support plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has a plugin architecture for extending the admin control panel and forum functionality.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source XMPP-based social platform combining chat, communities, blogs and video calls in one self-hostable app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Movim, started by Timothée Jaussoin in 2010, is a web-based social and communication platform built on the open XMPP protocol rather than a proprietary backend. It combines one-to-one and group chat, community 'Spaces', blog-style posts, 24-hour disappearing stories, and audio/video calling in a single progressive web app.
Because it's built on XMPP, a Movim account can interoperate with other XMPP clients such as Conversations and Dino, and instances can be run on standard XMPP servers like ejabberd or Prosody.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Privacy-conscious individuals, communities, classrooms and organizations that want a decentralized alternative to mainstream social platforms and are comfortable running or joining an XMPP-based server.

features.title: End-to-end encrypted chat
features.description: Supports OMEMO encryption for private one-to-one messaging.

features.title: Communities and Spaces
features.description: Group discussion areas for organizing communities around topics.

features.title: Blog publishing
features.description: Users can publish blog-style posts with a real-time feed.

features.title: Disappearing stories
features.description: Photo/text stories that expire after 24 hours.

features.title: Audio/video calling
features.description: One-to-one and group video calls with screen sharing.

features.title: XMPP interoperability
features.description: Works alongside other XMPP clients like Conversations and Dino, and runs on standard servers such as ejabberd or Prosody.

pros: Free and open source, built on an open decentralized protocol
Combines chat, blogging, communities and video calls in one app
Interoperates with the wider XMPP client ecosystem
Progressive web app installs on phones, tablets and desktops
Supports end-to-end encrypted messaging via OMEMO
cons: Requires an XMPP server (ejabberd/Prosody) to self-host
Smaller mainstream user base than commercial social networks
Project relies on donations for ongoing hosting and development

faqs.answer: Yes, Movim is free and open-source software; the project accepts optional donations to fund hosting and development.

faqs.question: What protocol does Movim use?
faqs.answer: It's built on XMPP, an open, decentralized messaging standard.

faqs.question: Can I use Movim with other XMPP clients?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Movim account works with other XMPP clients such as Conversations and Dino.

faqs.question: Does Movim support video calls?

use_cases.title: Unified XMPP client

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source PHP script for building self-hosted social networking sites, communities and membership platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OSSN (Open Source Social Network) is a PHP-based script that lets operators stand up their own branded social networking site, online community, membership platform or private collaboration space. It's distributed as free, open-source software with no artificial user limits or licensing fees for the core product.

The project provides user profiles, timelines, groups, messaging, notifications and photo albums out of the box, and can be extended through a component marketplace of add-ons and a hook/event system that avoids modifying core files.

enrichment.short_description: Decentralized, self-hosted microblogging ecosystem built on the plain-text twtxt protocol, with no central server or company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Yarn.social is a decentralized ecosystem for social micro-blogging built around the twtxt specification — a minimal plain-text format where each post is a single line containing a timestamp and a message, stored in a `twtxt.txt` file hosted wherever the author chooses.

There is no central Yarn.social server to sign up for. Instead, the project provides open-source software — including twtd (a single-user feed server) and yarnd (a multi-user 'pod' server) — that anyone can self-host to publish and follow feeds.

The ecosystem also bridges to other formats, including ActivityPub-based fediverse platforms and RSS/Atom feeds, and includes a search/crawler tool for discovering feeds across the network. It is created and maintained by James Mills (prologic), operator of the twtxt.net pod.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Yarn.social suits people who want to own their social posts as plain text on infrastructure they control, and developers or communities interested in a lightweight, decentralized alternative to mainstream microblogging platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted WebRTC gateway server from Meetecho for building custom real-time audio, video, and data applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Janus (Janus Gateway) is a general-purpose, open-source WebRTC server developed by Meetecho, written in C for a lightweight footprint. Rather than shipping as a finished conferencing app, it acts as middleware that relays RTP/RTCP media and JSON signaling between browsers and server-side logic.

Its architecture is plugin-based: Janus itself is protocol-agnostic, and specific capabilities — such as echo tests, conference bridges (videoroom), media recording/streaming, and SIP gateways — are provided by loadable server-side plugins, so deployments only include the functionality they need.

Because it is self-hosted and highly modular, Janus is commonly used as a building block for custom video conferencing, streaming, and real-time communication products rather than as an out-of-the-box end-user application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Janus is aimed at developers and organizations that need to build custom WebRTC-based communication features — video conferencing, streaming, or SIP integration — on infrastructure they control, rather than end users looking for a ready-made meeting app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk C2C ('cam-to-cam') is a self-hosted, open-source WebRTC video calling app built by Miroslav Pejic for one-to-one real-time communication. Unlike the broader MiroTalk conferencing products, each C2C room is limited to exactly two participants, which is used as a security and simplicity measure.

Calls are peer-to-peer and end-to-end encrypted, with screen sharing supported alongside camera video. The app requires only a room name to join and can be embedded into any website with a simple iframe.

It is part of the wider MiroTalk suite (which also includes P2P, SFU, and other products) and is released under the AGPLv3 license, with a commercial license also available for closed-source use via CodeCanyon.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MiroTalk C2C fits use cases that need a simple, private, two-person video call embedded into a site or app — such as 1:1 support calls, interviews, or telehealth-style consultations — run on self-hosted infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk P2P is a free, open-source, browser-based video conferencing app built on peer-to-peer WebRTC architecture, requiring no download, plugin, or login to join a call.

Because media flows directly between participants rather than through a media server, MiroTalk P2P is aimed at smaller calls where it can offer low latency and high video quality (up to 8K/60fps) with end-to-end encryption. It includes public and private chat, an interactive whiteboard, meeting recording, and file sharing over DTLS-encrypted transfer.
It is released under the AGPLv3 license and is self-hosted via Node.js or Docker; a commercial license is also available through CodeCanyon for closed-source use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MiroTalk P2P suits small teams or individuals who want a free, self-hosted, privacy-focused video calling tool for small-group calls without relying on a third-party SaaS provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk SFU is a free, open-source, browser-based video conferencing platform that uses Mediasoup as its Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU), allowing it to scale to larger group meetings than the peer-to-peer MiroTalk product.

It supports up to 8K/60fps video, screen sharing, echo-cancelled audio, chat, meeting recording, a collaborative whiteboard, file sharing, RTMP streaming, and virtual backgrounds, and offers optional ChatGPT integration and AI avatars. Media streams are encrypted with SRTP.
Like the rest of the MiroTalk suite, it is released under AGPLv3 for self-hosting, with a commercial license available via CodeCanyon, and exposes a REST API for building integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MiroTalk SFU suits organizations that need self-hosted group video conferencing at larger scale than a pure peer-to-peer tool can support, including teams that want streaming or recording built in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: plugNmeet is an open-source, self-hosted WebRTC conferencing platform maintained by Mynaparrot, built to be embedded into websites and applications rather than used only as a standalone app. Its backend is written in Go and runs on LiveKit and NATS, deploying as a single binary designed for horizontal scaling.

It offers HD recording, RTMP/WHIP streaming, real-time collaboration tools such as whiteboards and shared notepads, end-to-end encryption, and adaptive quality for varying network conditions. A newer 'AI Meeting Agent' feature adds live translation, transcription, and meeting summaries.
Because it is self-hosted and on-premise, plugNmeet is positioned as an alternative to third-party conferencing SaaS for organizations that want to keep meeting data under their own control.

paragraphs: plugNmeet suits businesses and platforms that want to embed customizable, self-hosted video conferencing directly into their own product, and organizations that need on-premise control over meeting data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ejabberd is an open-source messaging platform built in Erlang by ProcessOne, functioning as an XMPP server, MQTT broker, and SIP/WebRTC service for real-time communication. It has been developed by ProcessOne since 2002.

It supports over 400 XMPP extensions (including MUC group chat, MAM message archiving, PubSub, message carbons, push notifications, and OMEMO encryption), along with MQTT for IoT use cases, SIP/WebRTC for voice and video, and Matrix federation. Built-in clustering, REST APIs, web administration, and command-line tools are included.

The Community Server is free and open source under GPLv2. ProcessOne also offers a paid Business Edition with LTS support and enterprise modules, and Fluux, a managed SaaS hosting option. ejabberd is used at large scale by organizations including WhatsApp (in its early infrastructure) and others.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ejabberd suits organizations that need a robust, self-hosted messaging backend — for chat apps, IoT device messaging, or SIP/WebRTC voice and video — at scales ranging from small deployments to millions of concurrent connections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MongooseIM is a free, open-source instant messaging server built on Erlang by Erlang Solutions, a London-headquartered company founded in 1999 that also offers Erlang/OTP consulting and support services.

It functions primarily as an XMPP server, additionally exposing REST, MQTT, and AMQP interfaces, with a GraphQL admin API and TOML-based configuration. Features include end-to-end encryption support, group chat, delivery confirmations, push notifications via the companion MongoosePush service, and video/voice signaling coordination.

MongooseIM is designed for horizontal and vertical scaling across distributed data centers, and Erlang Solutions markets it toward organizations needing to support large numbers of concurrent connections, including in healthcare and fintech settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MongooseIM suits engineering teams that need a self-hosted, horizontally scalable XMPP-based messaging backend, particularly in regulated industries like healthcare and fintech where data control and GDPR compliance matter.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Openfire is a real-time collaboration server that implements the XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol) standard, released under the Apache License 2.0. The project began in 2002 as Jive Messenger, was renamed Wildfire in 2005, and became Openfire in 2007 after Jive Software handed it to the community; it is now maintained by the Ignite Realtime Foundation.

Administrators install Openfire on their own Windows, Linux, Unix, or Mac OS X servers and manage it through a browser-based admin console. It supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Firebird, and CockroachCB as backing databases, and its functionality can be extended through an official plugin catalog, including a REST API plugin for programmatic administration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Openfire suits IT teams and developers who need an internal, self-hosted messaging and presence server — for example, organizations that want group chat, one-to-one messaging, and multi-user chat rooms without routing traffic through a third-party service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Prosody is a modern XMPP communication server started by Matthew Wild in August 2008, with its first public release in December 2008. It is released under the MIT License and written in Lua, which keeps its codebase compact and makes it straightforward to write custom modules.

Prosody is designed to be simple to install and configure while still supporting server-to-server federation with other XMPP-compatible services. It powers, or forms part of the backend for, several well-known projects, including Jitsi Meet and Snikket.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Prosody fits sysadmins and developers who want a lean, self-hosted XMPP server for personal, family, or organizational messaging, or as a federation-capable backend component for another product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Snikket is an open-source messaging platform first previewed in early 2020 as a self-hosted alternative to centralized apps like WhatsApp or Telegram. It bundles existing open-source XMPP components — the Prosody server, an Android client based on Conversations, and an iOS client based on Siskin IM — into a package that's easy for a non-expert to run.

Rather than a single company controlling all accounts, Snikket is designed as a federated network: anyone can run their own server (or use a Snikket-hosted instance) while still being able to message people on other Snikket or XMPP servers. In March 2024 the project launched Snikket Hosting, an optional paid managed-hosting service for people who don't want to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Snikket targets families, friend groups, clubs, and small organizations that want private group messaging and calling without their data being monetized, whether they self-host or pay for managed hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Tigase XMPP Server is a Java-based, open-source XMPP/Jabber server started by Artur Hefczyc in October 2004. It's released under the AGPL-3.0 license, with Tigase, Inc. also offering a commercial license for organizations that can't use AGPL terms.

The project emphasizes performance and low footprint: its core binary is under 3MB, it can run with as little as 10MB of RAM for basic setups, and the vendor reports deployments handling large volumes of traffic with near-linear scalability across clustered nodes. Being JVM-based, it can be deployed on-premises or on any cloud provider that supports Java.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Tigase suits engineering teams building large-scale or high-uptime messaging infrastructure — from self-hosted community servers up to enterprise deployments needing clustering and detailed runtime metrics.

features.title: Modular Java architecture
features.description: A component-based design lets operators enable only the modules they need.

features.title: Clustering and load balancing
features.description: Supports distributed deployments with near-linear scalability and automatic cluster reconfiguration.

features.title: Administration tooling
features.description: Includes command-line tools, a web UI, and runtime performance metrics for monitoring the server.

features.title: Small footprint
features.description: Core binary under 3MB and capable of running with minimal RAM for lightweight deployments.

features.title: Dual licensing
features.description: Free under AGPL-3.0, with a commercial license option from Tigase, Inc. for closed-source use.

pros: Designed and tested for very large-scale deployments
Low resource requirements for smaller instances
Commercial support available through Tigase, Inc. for organizations that need it
Long track record dating back to 2004
cons: AGPL-3.0 licensing requires attention to compliance for SaaS/hosted use cases
Java-based stack may be heavier to operate than lighter alternatives for small hobby deployments
Commercial pricing is not published and requires contacting Tigase, Inc.

faqs.question: Is Tigase free?
faqs.answer: The core Tigase XMPP Server is free and open source under AGPL-3.0. Tigase, Inc. also sells a commercial license for cases where AGPL terms aren't suitable.

faqs.question: What platforms does Tigase run on?
faqs.answer: As a Java application, it runs anywhere a JVM is supported, including on-premises servers and major cloud providers.

faqs.question: Does Tigase support clustering?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's built for distributed deployments with load balancing and near-linear scalability.

use_cases.title: Large-scale messaging infrastructure
use_cases.description: Run a self-hosted XMPP backend intended to serve very large numbers of concurrent users.

use_cases.title: Embedded messaging in a product
use_cases.description: License Tigase commercially to embed XMPP messaging in a closed-source product.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tigase is an open-source, Java-based XMPP server built for large-scale, self-hosted messaging deployments, with AGPL and commercial licensing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, browser-based XMPP chat client that can run as a full-page app, embedded widget, or overlay on any site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Converse.js is a client-side XMPP chat application written in JavaScript, created by JC Brand starting in 2012 and released under the Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPLv2). Because it runs entirely in the browser, it can connect to any standards-compliant XMPP server, including Openfire, Prosody, and ejabberd, without vendor lock-in.

It supports direct messages, group chats (multi-user chatrooms), OMEMO end-to-end encryption, HTTP file upload, message archiving, and translations into more than 30 languages. Converse can be deployed as a full-page web app, embedded as a widget in an existing site, or overlaid as a chat popup, and it has been integrated into projects such as PeerTube, WordPress, and Roundcube.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Converse.js is aimed at developers who need to add real-time chat to a website or product and want a self-hosted, open-source client rather than a proprietary embedded widget.

features.title: Multiple deployment modes
features.description: Use it as a standalone full-page app, an embeddable widget, or an overlay chat window on an existing site.

features.title: OMEMO end-to-end encryption
features.description: Supports OMEMO for encrypted one-to-one and group conversations.

features.title: Multi-user chatrooms
features.description: Supports XMPP MUC group chats alongside direct messaging.

features.title: Broad server compatibility
features.description: Works with any XMPP server, including Openfire, Prosody, and ejabberd.

features.title: Wide translation coverage
features.description: Localized into more than 30 languages by the community.

features.title: File sharing
features.description: Supports HTTP file upload for sharing images and documents in chat.

pros: No server-side component required — runs entirely client-side against any XMPP server
Flexible embedding options (widget, overlay, or full app)
MPLv2 license avoids vendor lock-in
Already integrated into several other open-source projects
cons: Requires a separate XMPP server (e.g. Prosody or Openfire) to actually send messages
End users need some XMPP account/server setup, which is less turnkey than closed SaaS chat widgets
Official commercial support is provided by a separate consultancy (Opkode), not the core project

faqs.question: Is Converse.js free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open-source software under the Mozilla Public License 2.0.

faqs.question: Does Converse.js need its own server?
faqs.answer: No, it's a client only — it connects to any existing XMPP server such as Prosody, Openfire, or ejabberd.

faqs.question: Can I embed Converse.js in my website?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports being embedded as a widget or chat overlay, in addition to running as a standalone app.

use_cases.title: Add chat to a website
use_cases.description: Embed Converse.js as a widget or overlay to give visitors real-time chat backed by an XMPP server.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team or community chat UI
use_cases.description: Pair Converse.js with a self-hosted XMPP server like Prosody or Openfire for a fully self-hosted messaging stack.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Converse.js is a free, open-source, browser-based XMPP chat client that works with any XMPP server and embeds into any website.

enrichment.short_description: Decentralized, XMPP-based communication and collaboration suite (now renamed Libervia) with web, desktop, mobile, and CLI clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salut à Toi is a decentralized communication and collaboration project built on the XMPP protocol, maintained by a French nonprofit (association loi 1901) led by developer Goffi. As of version 0.8, the software itself was renamed Libervia — the association behind the project keeps the name Salut à Toi, but the project and its interfaces are now branded Libervia.

Most of the codebase is released under AGPL v3+. It goes beyond instant messaging to cover blogging (via Pubsub nodes), calendar events, photo albums, decentralized task lists and grocery lists, and file sharing, with interfaces for the web (Libervia Web), desktop, an experimental Android app, a CLI, and a terminal UI. It also offers an ActivityPub gateway to bridge XMPP conversations with the fediverse.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It suits users and developers who want a federated, self-hostable alternative to mainstream messaging and social platforms, and who need more than chat — such as shared calendars, blogs, or task lists — built on an open protocol they can run themselves.

features.title: End-to-end encrypted messaging
features.description: Supports OMEMO and OpenPGP encryption for one-to-one and group conversations.

features.title: Multi-user chat and calls
features.description: Group chat (MUC) plus one-on-one and multi-participant audio/video calls using peer-to-peer or SFU routing.

features.title: Pubsub-based blogging and calendars
features.description: Publish blog posts and calendar events with RSVP over XMPP Pubsub nodes.

features.title: Decentralized lists
features.description: Shared task lists, tickets, and grocery lists that sync across a user's devices and contacts.

features.title: ActivityPub gateway
features.description: Bridges XMPP conversations and content with fediverse platforms that speak ActivityPub.

features.title: Multiple client interfaces
features.description: Available as a web app (Libervia Web), desktop client, experimental Android app, CLI, and terminal UI.

pros: Goes well beyond chat into blogging, calendars, and file/task sharing on a single decentralized protocol
Multiple client types (web, desktop, CLI, TUI) for different workflows
AGPL-licensed and self-hostable
Built-in bridge to the ActivityPub fediverse
cons: Android support is still experimental and there's no stable iOS app
Smaller community and documentation base than mainstream XMPP clients
Project rename (Salut à Toi to Libervia) can make it harder to find current resources

faqs.question: Is Salut à Toi the same as Libervia?
faqs.answer: Yes. As of version 0.8, the project itself was renamed Libervia; the nonprofit association behind it retains the name Salut à Toi.

faqs.question: Is it free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, nearly all of the codebase is released under AGPL v3+ and is free to self-host.

faqs.question: Does it only do chat?
faqs.answer: No, it also supports Pubsub-based blogging, calendars, photo albums, task/grocery lists, and file sharing on top of XMPP.

use_cases.title: Federated personal communication
use_cases.description: Use it as a decentralized alternative to mainstream chat and social apps, hosted on your own or a trusted XMPP server.

use_cases.title: Lightweight collaboration
use_cases.description: Coordinate shared calendars, task lists, and blogs with a group over XMPP instead of separate SaaS tools.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Salut à Toi (now Libervia) is a free, open-source, decentralized XMPP suite for messaging, blogging, calendars, and file sharing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source management platform for Community Supported Agriculture groups, covering members, deliveries, and billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ACP Admin — also published under the name CSA Admin, since "ACP" (Agriculture Contractuelle de Proximité) is the French-language term for Community Supported Agriculture (CSA) — is a web application originally built by developer Thibaud Guillaume-Gentil to manage Rage de Vert, a CSA group in Neuchâtel, Switzerland. It has been used by organizations since 2014 and now serves more than 30 organizations across Switzerland, Germany, and the Netherlands, managing over 140,000 basket deliveries a year.

The source is released under the O'Saasy License, which permits free self-hosting for any purpose but reserves the right to resell the software as a competing hosted service to the original author. The maintainer also offers a managed hosting option with GDPR/nLPD-compliant infrastructure, backups, and updates included.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's built for CSA/ACP organizers — associations, cooperatives, or small businesses running subscription-based produce delivery — who need to manage members, waitlists, delivery schedules, invoicing, and a supplementary online shop in one system.

features.title: Member and waitlist management
features.description: Handles member registration, subscription management, and GDPR-compliant waitlists.

features.title: Delivery scheduling
features.description: Supports flexible delivery frequencies and basket content tracking.

features.title: Automated invoicing
features.description: Generates invoices with QR codes and tracks payments, including EBICS integration for Swiss bank reconciliation.

features.title: Annual renewal workflows
features.description: Simplifies the yearly process of renewing member subscriptions.

features.title: Online shop add-on
features.description: Sells supplementary products alongside regular baskets, with inventory tracking.

features.title: Member communication tools
features.description: Customizable email and newsletter tools for keeping members informed.

pros: Purpose-built for CSA/ACP operations rather than generic e-commerce
Free to self-host under the O'Saasy License
Managed hosting option removes the need to run infrastructure yourself
Proven at scale with 140,000+ baskets managed annually across multiple organizations
cons: Managed hosting pricing is usage-based (percentage of invoiced amount), which can be harder to budget than a flat fee
The O'Saasy License restricts reselling it as a competing hosted service
Maintained largely by a single developer rather than a company or large team

faqs.question: Is ACP Admin free?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting the software is free under the O'Saasy License. The maintainer's managed hosting service charges 1% of the amount invoiced through the platform per year, capped at 2,400 CHF/EUR, with no setup fees.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ACP Admin and CSA Admin?
faqs.answer: They are the same software; "ACP" is the French term used in French-speaking regions, while "CSA" is the English term for Community Supported Agriculture.

faqs.question: Can I self-host ACP Admin?
faqs.answer: Yes, the O'Saasy License explicitly permits free self-hosting; the maintainer's managed hosting is an optional paid alternative.

use_cases.title: Running a CSA/ACP subscription program
use_cases.description: Manage member subscriptions, delivery schedules, and basket invoicing for a produce cooperative.

use_cases.title: Multi-organization farm networks
use_cases.description: Coordinate several affiliated CSA groups on shared or self-hosted infrastructure.

features: Full source code available on GitHub

pricing_plans.price: 1% of amount invoiced per year, capped at 2,400 CHF/EUR

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ACP Admin (CSA Admin) is open-source software for managing Community Supported Agriculture groups: members, deliveries, and invoicing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source ordering and billing platform for food cooperatives, organic shops, and farm stores.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FoodCoopShop is an open-source web application, started by developer Mario Rothauer in 2014, for managing regional food orders from direct marketers. It's built on CakePHP and MySQL and is free to download and self-host, with Docker support for local development.

The software gives producers a dedicated login to manage their own product listings while giving cooperative members a shared ordering interface with support for different delivery schedules and an automated credit/balance billing system. According to the project's own site, over 50 initiatives are currently using it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's aimed at food cooperatives, organic stores (Bioläden), and farm shops (Hofläden) — mainly in German-speaking regions — that need collective ordering and producer coordination without building custom software.

features.title: Collective ordering
features.description: Members place orders from a shared catalog sourced from multiple producers.

features.title: Producer login area
features.description: Gives producers direct access to manage their own products and availability.

features.title: Flexible delivery schedules
features.description: Supports different delivery/pickup schedules per cooperative.

features.title: Credit/balance billing
features.description: Automates member billing through a running credit and balance system rather than per-order invoicing.

features.title: Docker-based dev environment
features.description: Ships with Docker tooling to simplify setting up a local development or self-hosted instance.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hostable under CakePHP/MySQL
Purpose-built for the specific workflow of food co-ops with multiple producers
Producer-facing login reduces manual coordination work
Active maintenance with at least one release per year
cons: Primarily documented and used in German-speaking markets, though English is supported
Self-hosting requires PHP/MySQL server administration skills
Maintained mainly by its original developer rather than a large team

faqs.question: Is FoodCoopShop free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source. Paid support and hosting services are available separately from the creator.

faqs.question: What is FoodCoopShop built with?
faqs.answer: It's built on CakePHP and MySQL, with Docker support for setting up a development environment.

faqs.question: Who is FoodCoopShop for?
faqs.answer: Food cooperatives, organic shops (Bioläden), and farm stores (Hofläden) that need collective ordering and producer coordination.

use_cases.title: Cooperative food ordering
use_cases.description: Run a shared online ordering system for a food cooperative sourcing from multiple local producers.

use_cases.title: Farm/organic shop management
use_cases.description: Give a farm shop or organic store an online ordering and billing system tailored to direct-marketing sales.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: FoodCoopShop is free, open-source software for food cooperatives and farm shops to manage collective ordering and producer billing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source web app for running a non-profit food cooperative: product catalog, ordering, accounting, and job scheduling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Foodsoft is an open-source, web-based application for running a non-profit food cooperative — a group of members who buy directly from suppliers of their own choosing, effectively operating a collective do-it-yourself supermarket. It's maintained on GitHub under the foodcoops organization and has a long history within the German and European food-coop community.

The software covers the core operational needs of a coop: maintaining a shared product catalog, taking member orders online, tracking accounting/balances, and scheduling the volunteer jobs members take on to keep the coop running. It can be self-hosted, and the project also lists third-party "Foodsoft Hosting" options for coops that don't want to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Foodsoft is built for member-run, non-profit food cooperatives that need to coordinate ordering, accounting, and volunteer job scheduling across a group of members rather than a traditional retail storefront.

features.title: Shared product catalog
features.description: Maintains a coop-wide catalog of products sourced from chosen suppliers.

features.title: Online member ordering
features.description: Members place and track their own orders through the web app.

features.title: Coop accounting
features.description: Tracks member balances and coop-level accounting tied to orders.

features.title: Volunteer job scheduling
features.description: Coordinates the shifts and tasks members take on as part of running the coop.

features.title: Self-hosted or hosted deployment
features.description: Can be run on your own infrastructure or through third-party Foodsoft Hosting providers.

pros: Free and open source, purpose-built for non-profit food coop operations
Covers ordering, accounting, and job scheduling in one system rather than several tools
Long-running project with an established community of food coops using it
Hosting options exist for coops that don't want to self-host
cons: Documentation and community are strongest in German-speaking regions
Self-hosting requires Ruby on Rails server administration knowledge
Volunteer-maintained pace of development can vary over time

faqs.question: Is Foodsoft free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Foodsoft is open-source software and free to self-host. Some third-party providers also offer paid Foodsoft Hosting for coops that don't want to manage a server.

faqs.question: What does Foodsoft manage?
faqs.answer: It manages a coop's product catalog, member ordering, accounting, and volunteer job scheduling.

faqs.question: Who maintains Foodsoft?
faqs.answer: It's maintained as a community open-source project on GitHub under the foodcoops organization.

use_cases.title: Running a member-owned food coop
use_cases.description: Coordinate product ordering, accounting, and volunteer scheduling for a non-profit, member-run food cooperative.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Foodsoft is free, open-source software for running a non-profit food cooperative: catalog, ordering, accounting, and job scheduling.
